content.text:
Good, I have the full output to assess. Here's my quality assessment:

---

## Quality Assessment

**What works well:**
- All 18 HIPAA PHI categories detected: SSN, MRN, phone, email, DOB, dates, addresses, names, providers, facilities, encounter IDs, accounts, health plan IDs, device IDs, NPI, DEA, URLs, IP addresses
- Guillemet format is clean and readable — `‹RED:CATEGORY text›` is easy to scan
- Structure preserved — sections, labeled fields, narrative all come through naturally
- Both fixtures produce meaningful output with no crashes

**Issues found — ranked by severity:**

### 1. False positive noise (YELLOW on common words)
- `‹YELLOW:NAME day›` — "day" matches a surname dictionary entry
- `‹YELLOW:NAME wall›` — "wall" matches a surname
- `‹YELLOW:NAME Ray›` — "X-Ray" split into tokens, "Ray" matches a name
- `‹YELLOW:NAME weeks›` — "weeks" matches a surname
- `‹YELLOW:NAME may›` — "may" matches a firstname

These are dictionary collisions the whitelist didn't catch. **₢A9AAN (entity-propagation-tier) won't fix this** — this is a whitelist gap, not a propagation problem. Fixable by adding "day", "wall", "ray", "weeks", "may" to `english_whitelist.txt` or `medical_whitelist.txt`.

### 2. Provider time fragments tagged as PROVIDER
- `‹RED:PROVIDER 18›:‹RED:PROVIDER 47›` — the time "18:47" after the provider signature is being tagged by Tier 2 anchored scanning. The anchored scan for "Electronically signed by:" bleeds past the provider name into the timestamp. Same for "19:02", "14:33".

### 3. Consulting label partial miss (geriatric)
- `Consulting: ‹YELLOW:NAME Naomi› Okwu, MD` — "Naomi" caught by dictionary (YELLOW) but "Okwu" missed entirely (not in surname dictionary, not anchored). "Consulting:" isn't in the anchored label list. However, at the signature line: `‹RED:PROVIDER Naomi› ‹RED:PROVIDER Okwu›` is correctly tagged RED. This is a label gap — "Consulting:" should probably be an anchor label, or ₢A9AAN entity propagation would catch "Naomi Okwu" in narrative after seeing it in the signature.

### 4. "husband"/"daughter" tagged as RED:NAME
- `(‹RED:NAME husband›)`, `(‹RED:NAME daughter›)` — The anchored scanner tags these as names because they follow the "Emergency Contact:" label. Not technically wrong (they're part of the relationship field), but semantically inaccurate — they're roles, not names.

### 5. Facility/city not propagated into narrative
- "Maine Medical Center" appears in narrative as untagged text (progress note HPI: "transported to Maine Medical Center")
- "Portland" tagged YELLOW:NAME in narrative (it's a city in the dictionary, matching as a name). This is exactly what ₢A9AAN is designed to fix.

### 6. Print ID partial address match
- `Print ID: RPT-2026-0408-‹RED:ADDRESS 18472›` — the trailing digits match an address/zip pattern. Not a real address, but harmless false positive.

---

## Relationship to remaining paces

| Issue | Pace that addresses it | Notes |
|-------|----------------------|-------|
| False positive common words (day, wall, ray, weeks, may) | **None** — needs a new pace or whitelist-tuning work | Could be a small standalone pace |
| Provider time fragments | **None** — anchored scanner boundary logic | Needs a fix in `apcrm_scan_anchored` |
| Consulting label gap | **₢A9AAN** (entity propagation) would partially fix | Adding "Consulting:" to anchored labels also helps |
| Facility not propagated | **₢A9AAN** (entity propagation) | This is exactly its purpose |
| SSA download | **₢A9AAM** (ssa-download-resolution) | Orthogonal |
| Real-world validation | **₢A9AAL** (deploy-and-real-data-test) | Would surface more issues like these |

**₢A9AAN is the most impactful remaining pace for assay quality** — it would catch unpropagated entities like "Maine Medical Center" and "Naomi Okwu" in narrative text.

The whitelist gaps and anchored-scanner time bleed are not covered by any existing pace. They're small fixes (whitelist additions, boundary logic tweak) that could be folded into a new pace or handled as pre-wrap polish on ₢A9AAO itself.

**Recommendation**: Do you want me to fix the whitelist gaps and time-bleed issue now (as part of ₢A9AAO before wrapping), or slate a new pace for detection tuning?

message.content: I know the prototype specification doesn't proscribe details about the algorithm performed here, and now I think that is a shortcoming.  Ought we start to identify ahd articulate defnitions there so that we can manage this right?

content.text: Good instinct. Let me read APCPS to see what's currently specified and where the gap is.

content.content: 1 # Ann's PHI Clipbuddy — Prototype Specification 2 3 ## Scope 4 5 This document specifies the engineering pipeline for the proof-of-life prototype. It covers detection algorithms, tech stack, data sources, project structure, and scope boundaries. It does NOT cover the user experience — see [APCS-Specification.md](APCS-Specification.md) for the UX plan. 6 7 This is a living document. The pipeline will evolve rapidly during prototype development. Changes here do not require parallel updates to the product spec. 8 9 ## Scope Boundaries 10 11 The prototype deliberately excludes: 12 13 - **No JavaScript** — All rendering logic is Rust. The Tauri webview is a passive display surface receiving complete HTML from the Rust backend. Inline `onclick` attributes use the Tauri bridge primitive (`window.__TAURI__.core.invoke`) for command dispatch only — no `.js` files, no JS application logic, no JS state management. This eliminates the language-boundary bug class (e.g., byte-offset vs char-offset mismatch at the Rust-JS serialization boundary). 14 - **No persistence** — no logs, no audit trail, no history of anonymized notes 15 - **One patient at a time** — no batch processing, no queue 16 - **No configuration** — no custom blacklists, no user preferences, no settings UI 17 - **No click-to-highlight** — clicking a finding does not scroll/highlight in the document preview (future feature) 18 - **No multi-word merge** — adjacent name hits are individually flagged, not merged into a single token 19 20 ## Tech Stack 21 22 | Layer | Crate | Version | License | Rationale | 23 |-------|-------|---------|---------|-----------| 24 | Clipboard | `arboard` | 3.x | MIT/Apache | Only crate with cross-platform HTML clipboard read (`get_html()`) | 25 | HTML parsing | `scraper` | latest | MIT | DOM + CSS selectors, built on Mozilla's html5ever | 26 | Dictionary matching | `aho-corasick` | latest | MIT/Unlicense | Single-pass multi-pattern O(n) regardless of dictionary size | 27 | Pattern matching | `regex` | latest | MIT/Apache | Structural PHI patterns | 28 | Word boundaries | `unicode-segmentation` | latest | MIT/Apache | UAX#29 compliant word boundary detection | 29 | GUI shell | `tauri` | 2.x | MIT | Rust-rendered HTML/CSS frontend, system webview as passive display | 30 | File watching | `notify` | latest | MIT/Apache | FSEvents on macOS, ReadDirectoryChanges on Windows | 31 32 All crates are pure Rust, fully Cargo-lockable on macOS and Windows. Tauri requires the system webview (WebKit on macOS, WebView2 on Windows — both ship with the OS). The webview receives pre-rendered HTML from Rust on every state change; no JavaScript application code exists in the project. 33 34 ### Tauri CLI cwd Constraint 35 36 The `cargo tauri build` command discovers its project by walking up from the current working directory looking for `Cargo.toml` and `tauri.conf.json`. It has no `--manifest-path` equivalent. Additionally, `tauri.conf.json` uses relative paths (e.g., `"frontendDist": "./ui"`) that resolve from the Tauri project directory. 37 38 This means `cargo tauri build` must run with cwd set to `Tools/apck/apcd/`. This is an external tool constraint, not a design choice. All other cargo commands (`cargo run`, `cargo test`) use `--manifest-path` and run from the project root per BCG convention. The two `cargo tauri` call sites (`apcc_build`, `apcc_deploy`) use BCG isolation subshells to contain the `cd`. 39 40 ## Input: Epic "Copy All" HTML 41 42 Epic's "Copy All" places HTML on the clipboard with structural formatting: 43 44 - `Label: value` patterns for demographics and metadata 45 - Section headers for clinical categories (Chief Complaint, HPI, PMH, Medications, Vitals, Labs, Assessment/Plan) 46 - Narrative freetext in the History of Present Illness and other sections 47 - Signature blocks with provider names and timestamps 48 49 The HTML structure is semi-structured — not arbitrary prose and not fully machine-readable. This structure is the primary advantage over plain text processing: labels anchor PHI detection with high precision. 50 51 ## Clipboard Handling 52 53 ### Clinical Content Heuristic 54 55 Before processing, the engine checks whether clipboard content appears to be clinical. The check scans for known Epic/clinical label patterns: 56 57 **Positive indicators** (presence of 2+ triggers acceptance): 58 - `Patient:`, `DOB:`, `MRN:`, `Attending:`, `Facility:` 59 - `Chief Complaint:`, `History of Present Illness:`, `Assessment/Plan:` 60 - `Vitals:`, `Labs:`, `Medications:`, `Allergies:` 61 - HTML `Label:` structure characteristic of Epic formatting 62 63 If clinical content is detected, the engine proceeds to the detection pipeline and clears the system clipboard (writes empty string via `arboard::set_text("")`). 64 65 If clinical content is NOT detected, the app clears any previous triage state and returns to the initial instruction view. The app displays a brief diagnostic line showing: clipboard content length, content type (HTML/plain text), and the first ~100 characters of content. This diagnostic aids debugging when real Epic data fails the heuristic — the developer can see what was on the clipboard and update the label list accordingly. 66 67 This heuristic will be refined when tested against real Epic output. The initial label list is calibrated to pass the synthetic test fixture. 68 69 ### Clipboard Change Detection 70 71 On window focus, the engine compares the current clipboard content (byte-for-byte string comparison) against the last successfully consumed content. If identical, the existing triage state is preserved — no reprocessing. If different, the clinical content heuristic runs on the new content. Clinical notes are a few KB; storing the last consumed string for comparison has negligible memory cost at prototype scale. 72 73 ## Detection Pipeline 74 75 ### Overview 76 77 Three tiers execute in sequence. Results merge by highest severity — if a token is flagged by multiple tiers, the most severe classification wins. 78 79 ``` 80 Input: Epic HTML clipboard content 81 | 82 Parse HTML → typed spans with labels and positions 83 | 84 Tier 1: Regex scan → RED 85 | 86 Tier 2: Label-anchored extraction → RED 87 | 88 Tier 3: Dictionary blacklist/whitelist scan → YELLOW or PASS 89 | 90 Merge by highest severity 91 | 92 Output: classified token stream → frontend 93 ``` 94 95 ### Tier 1 — Regex Patterns (→ RED) 96 97 Structural patterns with low false positive rate. No database dependency. 98 99 **Known ambiguities:** The zip code regex (`\d{5}`) can match 5-digit lab values, identifiers, or dosage amounts. The date regex can match version numbers or other formatted numerics. The street address regex can match non-address text with number-word-suffix patterns. These are accepted risks for the prototype — the conservative bias (false positive > false negative) means these appear as RED items the clinician can review. Context-aware refinement is deferred. 100 101 | Pattern | Category | Regex (representative) | Notes | 102 |---------|----------|----------------------|-------| 103 | SSN | `[SSN]` | `\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}` | | 104 | Phone | `[PHONE]` | `\d{3}[-.)]\s?\d{3}[-.)]\s?\d{4}`, `\(\d{3}\)\s?\d{3}-\d{4}` | Multiple formats | 105 | Email | `[EMAIL]` | `[\w.+-]+@[\w-]+\.[\w.-]+` | | 106 | Dates | `[DATE]` | `\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{2,4}`, `\d{1,2}-\d{1,2}-\d{2,4}`, month name patterns | Multiple formats | 107 | Street address | `[ADDRESS]` | `\d+\s+[A-Z]\w+\s+(St\|Dr\|Ave\|Blvd\|Rd\|Ln\|Way\|Ct\|Pl\|Cir\|Drive\|Street\|Avenue\|Boulevard\|Road\|Lane\|Court\|Place\|Circle)` | Followed by optional city/state/zip | 108 | Zip code | `[ADDRESS]` | `\b\d{5}(-\d{4})?\b` | Within address context | 109 | MRN (labeled) | `[MRN]` | Value following `MRN:` label | | 110 | Account (labeled) | `[ACCOUNT]` | Value following `Account` or `Acct` label | | 111 | Encounter ID (labeled) | `[ENCOUNTER_ID]` | Value following `Encounter ID:` label | | 112 113 Dates receive special handling: when a date follows a `DOB:` label, the engine computes age from the current date and emits `Age: NN` instead of `[DATE]` in the anonymized output. Per HIPAA Safe Harbor (45 CFR 164.514(b)(2)(i)(C)), ages over 89 are aggregated: computed age >89 emits `Age: 90+`. 114 115 ### Tier 2 — Label-Anchored Extraction (→ RED) 116 117 Words immediately following known Epic labels are PHI regardless of dictionary membership. The label is authoritative — if Epic says `Patient: Xylophone McZephyr`, that's a name even if no database contains it. 118 119 | Label Pattern | Category | Extraction Rule | 120 |--------------|----------|-----------------| 121 | `Patient:` | `[NAME]` | All text until next label or line break | 122 | `Attending:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break | 123 | `Provider:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break | 124 | `Referring:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break | 125 | `Facility:` | `[FACILITY]` | All text until next label or line break | 126 | `Electronically signed by:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break | 127 | `Emergency contact` | `[NAME]` | Name portion, phone extracted by Tier 1 | 128 | `primary care physician` | `[PROVIDER]` | Following name (e.g., "Dr. Susan Chen") | 129 130 Label matching is case-insensitive on the label text. Value extraction extends to the next structural boundary (label, line break, or HTML element boundary). 131 132 **Per-word anchoring:** Each word in the extracted value is individually flagged as RED. "Margaret J. Thornton" after `Patient:` produces three separate **anchored** items, each classified `[NAME]`. In anonymized output: `[NAME] [NAME] [NAME]`. No multi-word merge is attempted. 133 134 Items caught by Tier 2 are **anchored** — their RED classification is final. They are not subsequently dictionary-checked. If a word is both anchored (Tier 2) and matched (Tier 3), the anchored classification takes precedence. 135 136 ### Vocabulary: Anchored vs. Matched 137 138 Two independent mechanisms flag words as potential PHI. To avoid confusion, the spec distinguishes: 139 140 - **Anchored**: Flagged by Tier 2 because of *position* — the word follows a known label. Always RED. Independent of any dictionary. 141 - **Matched**: Flagged by Tier 3 because of *identity* — the word appears in a name/location dictionary. YELLOW (unless also anchored, in which case RED wins). 142 143 A word can be both anchored and matched (e.g., "Thornton" after `Patient:` AND in the Census surname list). Anchored always takes precedence. The term "blacklist" in this spec refers specifically to the Tier 3 name/location dictionaries; Tier 2 label-anchored extraction is a separate mechanism. 144 145 ### Tier 3 — Dictionary Blacklist/Whitelist (→ YELLOW or PASS) 146 147 Catches names and locations in narrative text that lack label anchors (e.g., "her husband Robert Thornton called 911"). 148 149 **Case handling:** All dictionary lookups are case-insensitive. The aho-corasick automaton is built from lowercase dictionary entries. Input tokens are lowercased before matching. Original case is preserved in display and anonymized output. 150 151 **Process:** 152 1. Segment text into words using `unicode-segmentation` (UAX#29 boundaries) 153 2. Lowercase each token for lookup; preserve original for display 154 3. Run aho-corasick automaton against lowercased tokens (single pass, O(n)) 155 4. For each hit, check whitelist membership (also lowercase) 156 5. Classify: 157 - Blacklist hit, no whitelist hit → **YELLOW** (matched, default ELIDE) 158 - Blacklist hit AND whitelist hit → **YELLOW** (collision, default ELIDE) 159 - Whitelist hit only → **PASS** 160 - Neither list → **PASS** 161 162 **Collision resolution context signals** (applied within YELLOW to aid clinician review): 163 - Capitalization mid-sentence suggests a proper noun (name) rather than common word 164 - Adjacent blacklist hits suggest a full name ("Robert Thornton" — two hits together) 165 - Section context: names in demographics header vs. narrative HPI carry different weight 166 167 These signals inform the findings panel display but do not change the YELLOW classification in the prototype. The clinician makes the final call. 168 169 ## Dictionary Data 170 171 ### Proof-of-Life Sizing 172 173 Curated-small dictionaries that prove the architecture without requiring full datasets: 174 175 | Dictionary | Source | License | Size | File | 176 |------------|--------|---------|------|------| 177 | Surnames | US Census 2010, top 1,000 by frequency | Public domain | ~1,000 entries | `dictionaries/surnames.txt` | 178 | First names | SSA baby names, top 1,000 | Public domain | ~1,000 entries | `dictionaries/firstnames.txt` | 179 | Cities | US state capitals + top 100 cities by population | Public domain | ~150 entries | `dictionaries/cities.txt` | 180 | Medical whitelist | Hand-curated: common medications, lab tests, diagnoses, anatomy, abbreviations | Original curation | ~500 entries | `dictionaries/medical_whitelist.txt` | 181 | English whitelist | Top 5,000 common English words | Public domain | ~5,000 entries | `dictionaries/english_whitelist.txt` | 182 183 Total: ~8,000 entries, ~80KB on disk. 184 185 ### Full Dataset Sizing (Future) 186 187 | Dictionary | Source | License | Size | 188 |------------|--------|---------|------| 189 | Surnames | US Census 2010, complete | Public domain | ~160,000 | 190 | First names | SSA baby names, complete | Public domain | ~100,000 | 191 | Cities | US Census incorporated places | Public domain | ~30,000 | 192 | Medical whitelist | SNOMED-CT + RxNorm + LOINC subsets | NLM license (redistribution constraints) | ~100,000+ | 193 | English whitelist | Full dictionary | Public domain | ~170,000 | 194 195 ## Anonymization 196 197 ### Placeholder Strategy 198 199 Category-specific placeholders preserve semantic context for Open Evidence: 200 201 | PHI Category | Placeholder | Example | 202 |-------------|-------------|---------| 203 | Patient/person names | `[NAME]` | "Ms. [NAME] is a 74-year-old female" | 204 | Dates (non-DOB) | `[DATE]` | "Visit Date: [DATE]" | 205 | Date of birth | `Age: NN` or `Age: 90+` | "DOB: 03/15/1952" → "Age: 74"; ages >89 → "Age: 90+" | 206 | Medical record number | `[MRN]` | "MRN: [MRN]" | 207 | Phone numbers | `[PHONE]` | "phone: [PHONE]" | 208 | Addresses | `[ADDRESS]` | "at [ADDRESS]" | 209 | Email addresses | `[EMAIL]` | | 210 | Social Security numbers | `[SSN]` | | 211 | Facility names | `[FACILITY]` | "Seen at [FACILITY]" | 212 | Provider names | `[PROVIDER]` | "Dr. [PROVIDER]" | 213 | Account numbers | `[ACCOUNT]` | | 214 | Encounter IDs | `[ENCOUNTER_ID]` | | 215 216 ### Output Format 217 218 Plain text with preserved section structure (line breaks, section headers). HTML formatting is stripped — this is itself a privacy measure, removing embedded metadata and hidden fields. The output should be readable when pasted into Open Evidence's chat input. 219 220 ## Self-Update Mechanism 221 222 The application watches `/Users/Shared/apcua/` for new `.app` bundles using the `notify` crate (FSEvents backend on macOS). On detection: 223 224 1. Copy new bundle over current application path 225 2. Spawn `open -n ` to launch new version 226 3. `std::process::exit(0)` to terminate current process 227 228 No confirmation dialog. The watcher runs on a background thread independent of the main Tauri event loop. If the watch directory does not exist at startup, the app creates it silently. 229 230 ## Project Structure 231 232 ``` 233 Tools/apck/ 234 apck-claude-context.md # Build/run/deploy instructions (RCG reference) 235 APCS-Specification.md # Product spec (UX vision) 236 APCPS-PrototypeSpecification.md # This document (pipeline engineering) 237 apcw_workbench.sh # Bash workbench 238 apcz_zipper.sh # Zipper enrollment 239 test_fixtures/ 240 epic_progress_note.html # Synthetic Epic clipboard data 241 apcd/ # Rust/Tauri source directory 242 Cargo.toml # Two [[bin]]: apcap (app), apcal (fixture loader) 243 src/ 244 apcap_main.rs # Tauri app entry point 245 apcal_main.rs # Fixture loader entry point 246 lib.rs # Engine library + test module declarations 247 apcre_engine.rs # PHI detection orchestrator 248 apcte_engine.rs # Tests for engine 249 apcrp_parse.rs # HTML clipboard parsing 250 apctp_parse.rs # Tests for parser 251 apcrm_match.rs # Dictionary/regex matching 252 apctm_match.rs # Tests for matching 253 apcrd_dictionaries.rs # Dictionary loading 254 apctd_dictionaries.rs # Tests for dictionaries 255 apcru_update.rs # Directory watcher + self-update (no unit tests) 256 ui/ 257 index.html 258 style.css 259 app.js 260 dictionaries/ 261 surnames.txt 262 firstnames.txt 263 cities.txt 264 medical_whitelist.txt 265 english_whitelist.txt 266 ``` 267 268 ## Prefix Tree 269 270 ``` 271 apc (non-terminal) 272 ├── apca (non-terminal) 273 │ ├── apcal — App Loader binary (fixture clipboard tool) 274 │ └── apcap — App Prototype binary (Tauri main) 275 ├── apcd — Rust/Tauri source directory 276 ├── apck — kit directory 277 ├── apcps — prototype specification document 278 ├── apcs — product specification document 279 ├── apcu (non-terminal) 280 │ └── apcua — update staging directory (/Users/Shared/apcua/) 281 ├── apcw — workbench 282 └── apcz — zipper 283 ``` 284 285 Rust source file prefixes follow RCG: `{cipher}r{classifier}_{name}.rs` where cipher is `apc`. Test files: `{cipher}t{classifier}_{name}.rs` — classifier matches between source and test. See RCG (Rust Coding Guide) for full naming conventions, including `z`-prefix internal declarations, string boundary discipline, and constant discipline. 286 287 `lib.rs` wiring: 288 ```rust 289 pub mod apcre_engine; 290 pub mod apcrp_parse; 291 pub mod apcrm_match; 292 pub mod apcrd_dictionaries; 293 pub mod apcru_update; 294 295 #[cfg(test)] mod apcte_engine; 296 #[cfg(test)] mod apctp_parse; 297 #[cfg(test)] mod apctm_match; 298 #[cfg(test)] mod apctd_dictionaries; 299 ``` 300 301 ## Tabtargets 302 303 | Tabtarget | Colophon | Purpose | 304 |-----------|----------|---------| 305 | `tt/apcw-b.Build.sh` | `apcw-b` | `cargo tauri build` (release) | 306 | `tt/apcw-r.Run.sh` | `apcw-r` | `cargo tauri dev` (local development) | 307 | `tt/apcw-d.Deploy.sh` | `apcw-d` | Build + scp to `anns-macbook-air:/Users/Shared/apcua/` | 308 | `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.sh` | `apcw-fl` | Run `apcal` to load fixture HTML onto clipboard | 309 | `tt/apcw-t.Test.sh` | `apcw-t` | `cargo test` in `apcd/` | 310

file.content: # Ann's PHI Clipbuddy — Prototype Specification ## Scope This document specifies the engineering pipeline for the proof-of-life prototype. It covers detection algorithms, tech stack, data sources, project structure, and scope boundaries. It does NOT cover the user experience — see [APCS-Specification.md](APCS-Specification.md) for the UX plan. This is a living document. The pipeline will evolve rapidly during prototype development. Changes here do not require parallel updates to the product spec. ## Scope Boundaries The prototype deliberately excludes: - **No JavaScript** — All rendering logic is Rust. The Tauri webview is a passive display surface receiving complete HTML from the Rust backend. Inline `onclick` attributes use the Tauri bridge primitive (`window.__TAURI__.core.invoke`) for command dispatch only — no `.js` files, no JS application logic, no JS state management. This eliminates the language-boundary bug class (e.g., byte-offset vs char-offset mismatch at the Rust-JS serialization boundary). - **No persistence** — no logs, no audit trail, no history of anonymized notes - **One patient at a time** — no batch processing, no queue - **No configuration** — no custom blacklists, no user preferences, no settings UI - **No click-to-highlight** — clicking a finding does not scroll/highlight in the document preview (future feature) - **No multi-word merge** — adjacent name hits are individually flagged, not merged into a single token ## Tech Stack | Layer | Crate | Version | License | Rationale | |-------|-------|---------|---------|-----------| | Clipboard | `arboard` | 3.x | MIT/Apache | Only crate with cross-platform HTML clipboard read (`get_html()`) | | HTML parsing | `scraper` | latest | MIT | DOM + CSS selectors, built on Mozilla's html5ever | | Dictionary matching | `aho-corasick` | latest | MIT/Unlicense | Single-pass multi-pattern O(n) regardless of dictionary size | | Pattern matching | `regex` | latest | MIT/Apache | Structural PHI patterns | | Word boundaries | `unicode-segmentation` | latest | MIT/Apache | UAX#29 compliant word boundary detection | | GUI shell | `tauri` | 2.x | MIT | Rust-rendered HTML/CSS frontend, system webview as passive display | | File watching | `notify` | latest | MIT/Apache | FSEvents on macOS, ReadDirectoryChanges on Windows | All crates are pure Rust, fully Cargo-lockable on macOS and Windows. Tauri requires the system webview (WebKit on macOS, WebView2 on Windows — both ship with the OS). The webview receives pre-rendered HTML from Rust on every state change; no JavaScript application code exists in the project. ### Tauri CLI cwd Constraint The `cargo tauri build` command discovers its project by walking up from the current working directory looking for `Cargo.toml` and `tauri.conf.json`. It has no `--manifest-path` equivalent. Additionally, `tauri.conf.json` uses relative paths (e.g., `"frontendDist": "./ui"`) that resolve from the Tauri project directory. This means `cargo tauri build` must run with cwd set to `Tools/apck/apcd/`. This is an external tool constraint, not a design choice. All other cargo commands (`cargo run`, `cargo test`) use `--manifest-path` and run from the project root per BCG convention. The two `cargo tauri` call sites (`apcc_build`, `apcc_deploy`) use BCG isolation subshells to contain the `cd`. ## Input: Epic "Copy All" HTML Epic's "Copy All" places HTML on the clipboard with structural formatting: - `Label: value` patterns for demographics and metadata - Section headers for clinical categories (Chief Complaint, HPI, PMH, Medications, Vitals, Labs, Assessment/Plan) - Narrative freetext in the History of Present Illness and other sections - Signature blocks with provider names and timestamps The HTML structure is semi-structured — not arbitrary prose and not fully machine-readable. This structure is the primary advantage over plain text processing: labels anchor PHI detection with high precision. ## Clipboard Handling ### Clinical Content Heuristic Before processing, the engine checks whether clipboard content appears to be clinical. The check scans for known Epic/clinical label patterns: **Positive indicators** (presence of 2+ triggers acceptance): - `Patient:`, `DOB:`, `MRN:`, `Attending:`, `Facility:` - `Chief Complaint:`, `History of Present Illness:`, `Assessment/Plan:` - `Vitals:`, `Labs:`, `Medications:`, `Allergies:` - HTML `Label:` structure characteristic of Epic formatting If clinical content is detected, the engine proceeds to the detection pipeline and clears the system clipboard (writes empty string via `arboard::set_text("")`). If clinical content is NOT detected, the app clears any previous triage state and returns to the initial instruction view. The app displays a brief diagnostic line showing: clipboard content length, content type (HTML/plain text), and the first ~100 characters of content. This diagnostic aids debugging when real Epic data fails the heuristic — the developer can see what was on the clipboard and update the label list accordingly. This heuristic will be refined when tested against real Epic output. The initial label list is calibrated to pass the synthetic test fixture. ### Clipboard Change Detection On window focus, the engine compares the current clipboard content (byte-for-byte string comparison) against the last successfully consumed content. If identical, the existing triage state is preserved — no reprocessing. If different, the clinical content heuristic runs on the new content. Clinical notes are a few KB; storing the last consumed string for comparison has negligible memory cost at prototype scale. ## Detection Pipeline ### Overview Three tiers execute in sequence. Results merge by highest severity — if a token is flagged by multiple tiers, the most severe classification wins. ``` Input: Epic HTML clipboard content | Parse HTML → typed spans with labels and positions | Tier 1: Regex scan → RED | Tier 2: Label-anchored extraction → RED | Tier 3: Dictionary blacklist/whitelist scan → YELLOW or PASS | Merge by highest severity | Output: classified token stream → frontend ``` ### Tier 1 — Regex Patterns (→ RED) Structural patterns with low false positive rate. No database dependency. **Known ambiguities:** The zip code regex (`\d{5}`) can match 5-digit lab values, identifiers, or dosage amounts. The date regex can match version numbers or other formatted numerics. The street address regex can match non-address text with number-word-suffix patterns. These are accepted risks for the prototype — the conservative bias (false positive > false negative) means these appear as RED items the clinician can review. Context-aware refinement is deferred. | Pattern | Category | Regex (representative) | Notes | |---------|----------|----------------------|-------| | SSN | `[SSN]` | `\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}` | | | Phone | `[PHONE]` | `\d{3}[-.)]\s?\d{3}[-.)]\s?\d{4}`, `\(\d{3}\)\s?\d{3}-\d{4}` | Multiple formats | | Email | `[EMAIL]` | `[\w.+-]+@[\w-]+\.[\w.-]+` | | | Dates | `[DATE]` | `\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{2,4}`, `\d{1,2}-\d{1,2}-\d{2,4}`, month name patterns | Multiple formats | | Street address | `[ADDRESS]` | `\d+\s+[A-Z]\w+\s+(St\|Dr\|Ave\|Blvd\|Rd\|Ln\|Way\|Ct\|Pl\|Cir\|Drive\|Street\|Avenue\|Boulevard\|Road\|Lane\|Court\|Place\|Circle)` | Followed by optional city/state/zip | | Zip code | `[ADDRESS]` | `\b\d{5}(-\d{4})?\b` | Within address context | | MRN (labeled) | `[MRN]` | Value following `MRN:` label | | | Account (labeled) | `[ACCOUNT]` | Value following `Account` or `Acct` label | | | Encounter ID (labeled) | `[ENCOUNTER_ID]` | Value following `Encounter ID:` label | | Dates receive special handling: when a date follows a `DOB:` label, the engine computes age from the current date and emits `Age: NN` instead of `[DATE]` in the anonymized output. Per HIPAA Safe Harbor (45 CFR 164.514(b)(2)(i)(C)), ages over 89 are aggregated: computed age >89 emits `Age: 90+`. ### Tier 2 — Label-Anchored Extraction (→ RED) Words immediately following known Epic labels are PHI regardless of dictionary membership. The label is authoritative — if Epic says `Patient: Xylophone McZephyr`, that's a name even if no database contains it. | Label Pattern | Category | Extraction Rule | |--------------|----------|-----------------| | `Patient:` | `[NAME]` | All text until next label or line break | | `Attending:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break | | `Provider:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break | | `Referring:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break | | `Facility:` | `[FACILITY]` | All text until next label or line break | | `Electronically signed by:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break | | `Emergency contact` | `[NAME]` | Name portion, phone extracted by Tier 1 | | `primary care physician` | `[PROVIDER]` | Following name (e.g., "Dr. Susan Chen") | Label matching is case-insensitive on the label text. Value extraction extends to the next structural boundary (label, line break, or HTML element boundary). **Per-word anchoring:** Each word in the extracted value is individually flagged as RED. "Margaret J. Thornton" after `Patient:` produces three separate **anchored** items, each classified `[NAME]`. In anonymized output: `[NAME] [NAME] [NAME]`. No multi-word merge is attempted. Items caught by Tier 2 are **anchored** — their RED classification is final. They are not subsequently dictionary-checked. If a word is both anchored (Tier 2) and matched (Tier 3), the anchored classification takes precedence. ### Vocabulary: Anchored vs. Matched Two independent mechanisms flag words as potential PHI. To avoid confusion, the spec distinguishes: - **Anchored**: Flagged by Tier 2 because of *position* — the word follows a known label. Always RED. Independent of any dictionary. - **Matched**: Flagged by Tier 3 because of *identity* — the word appears in a name/location dictionary. YELLOW (unless also anchored, in which case RED wins). A word can be both anchored and matched (e.g., "Thornton" after `Patient:` AND in the Census surname list). Anchored always takes precedence. The term "blacklist" in this spec refers specifically to the Tier 3 name/location dictionaries; Tier 2 label-anchored extraction is a separate mechanism. ### Tier 3 — Dictionary Blacklist/Whitelist (→ YELLOW or PASS) Catches names and locations in narrative text that lack label anchors (e.g., "her husband Robert Thornton called 911"). **Case handling:** All dictionary lookups are case-insensitive. The aho-corasick automaton is built from lowercase dictionary entries. Input tokens are lowercased before matching. Original case is preserved in display and anonymized output. **Process:** 1. Segment text into words using `unicode-segmentation` (UAX#29 boundaries) 2. Lowercase each token for lookup; preserve original for display 3. Run aho-corasick automaton against lowercased tokens (single pass, O(n)) 4. For each hit, check whitelist membership (also lowercase) 5. Classify: - Blacklist hit, no whitelist hit → **YELLOW** (matched, default ELIDE) - Blacklist hit AND whitelist hit → **YELLOW** (collision, default ELIDE) - Whitelist hit only → **PASS** - Neither list → **PASS** **Collision resolution context signals** (applied within YELLOW to aid clinician review): - Capitalization mid-sentence suggests a proper noun (name) rather than common word - Adjacent blacklist hits suggest a full name ("Robert Thornton" — two hits together) - Section context: names in demographics header vs. narrative HPI carry different weight These signals inform the findings panel display but do not change the YELLOW classification in the prototype. The clinician makes the final call. ## Dictionary Data ### Proof-of-Life Sizing Curated-small dictionaries that prove the architecture without requiring full datasets: | Dictionary | Source | License | Size | File | |------------|--------|---------|------|------| | Surnames | US Census 2010, top 1,000 by frequency | Public domain | ~1,000 entries | `dictionaries/surnames.txt` | | First names | SSA baby names, top 1,000 | Public domain | ~1,000 entries | `dictionaries/firstnames.txt` | | Cities | US state capitals + top 100 cities by population | Public domain | ~150 entries | `dictionaries/cities.txt` | | Medical whitelist | Hand-curated: common medications, lab tests, diagnoses, anatomy, abbreviations | Original curation | ~500 entries | `dictionaries/medical_whitelist.txt` | | English whitelist | Top 5,000 common English words | Public domain | ~5,000 entries | `dictionaries/english_whitelist.txt` | Total: ~8,000 entries, ~80KB on disk. ### Full Dataset Sizing (Future) | Dictionary | Source | License | Size | |------------|--------|---------|------| | Surnames | US Census 2010, complete | Public domain | ~160,000 | | First names | SSA baby names, complete | Public domain | ~100,000 | | Cities | US Census incorporated places | Public domain | ~30,000 | | Medical whitelist | SNOMED-CT + RxNorm + LOINC subsets | NLM license (redistribution constraints) | ~100,000+ | | English whitelist | Full dictionary | Public domain | ~170,000 | ## Anonymization ### Placeholder Strategy Category-specific placeholders preserve semantic context for Open Evidence: | PHI Category | Placeholder | Example | |-------------|-------------|---------| | Patient/person names | `[NAME]` | "Ms. [NAME] is a 74-year-old female" | | Dates (non-DOB) | `[DATE]` | "Visit Date: [DATE]" | | Date of birth | `Age: NN` or `Age: 90+` | "DOB: 03/15/1952" → "Age: 74"; ages >89 → "Age: 90+" | | Medical record number | `[MRN]` | "MRN: [MRN]" | | Phone numbers | `[PHONE]` | "phone: [PHONE]" | | Addresses | `[ADDRESS]` | "at [ADDRESS]" | | Email addresses | `[EMAIL]` | | | Social Security numbers | `[SSN]` | | | Facility names | `[FACILITY]` | "Seen at [FACILITY]" | | Provider names | `[PROVIDER]` | "Dr. [PROVIDER]" | | Account numbers | `[ACCOUNT]` | | | Encounter IDs | `[ENCOUNTER_ID]` | | ### Output Format Plain text with preserved section structure (line breaks, section headers). HTML formatting is stripped — this is itself a privacy measure, removing embedded metadata and hidden fields. The output should be readable when pasted into Open Evidence's chat input. ## Self-Update Mechanism The application watches `/Users/Shared/apcua/` for new `.app` bundles using the `notify` crate (FSEvents backend on macOS). On detection: 1. Copy new bundle over current application path 2. Spawn `open -n ` to launch new version 3. `std::process::exit(0)` to terminate current process No confirmation dialog. The watcher runs on a background thread independent of the main Tauri event loop. If the watch directory does not exist at startup, the app creates it silently. ## Project Structure ``` Tools/apck/ apck-claude-context.md # Build/run/deploy instructions (RCG reference) APCS-Specification.md # Product spec (UX vision) APCPS-PrototypeSpecification.md # This document (pipeline engineering) apcw_workbench.sh # Bash workbench apcz_zipper.sh # Zipper enrollment test_fixtures/ epic_progress_note.html # Synthetic Epic clipboard data apcd/ # Rust/Tauri source directory Cargo.toml # Two [[bin]]: apcap (app), apcal (fixture loader) src/ apcap_main.rs # Tauri app entry point apcal_main.rs # Fixture loader entry point lib.rs # Engine library + test module declarations apcre_engine.rs # PHI detection orchestrator apcte_engine.rs # Tests for engine apcrp_parse.rs # HTML clipboard parsing apctp_parse.rs # Tests for parser apcrm_match.rs # Dictionary/regex matching apctm_match.rs # Tests for matching apcrd_dictionaries.rs # Dictionary loading apctd_dictionaries.rs # Tests for dictionaries apcru_update.rs # Directory watcher + self-update (no unit tests) ui/ index.html style.css app.js dictionaries/ surnames.txt firstnames.txt cities.txt medical_whitelist.txt english_whitelist.txt ``` ## Prefix Tree ``` apc (non-terminal) ├── apca (non-terminal) │ ├── apcal — App Loader binary (fixture clipboard tool) │ └── apcap — App Prototype binary (Tauri main) ├── apcd — Rust/Tauri source directory ├── apck — kit directory ├── apcps — prototype specification document ├── apcs — product specification document ├── apcu (non-terminal) │ └── apcua — update staging directory (/Users/Shared/apcua/) ├── apcw — workbench └── apcz — zipper ``` Rust source file prefixes follow RCG: `{cipher}r{classifier}_{name}.rs` where cipher is `apc`. Test files: `{cipher}t{classifier}_{name}.rs` — classifier matches between source and test. See RCG (Rust Coding Guide) for full naming conventions, including `z`-prefix internal declarations, string boundary discipline, and constant discipline. `lib.rs` wiring: ```rust pub mod apcre_engine; pub mod apcrp_parse; pub mod apcrm_match; pub mod apcrd_dictionaries; pub mod apcru_update; #[cfg(test)] mod apcte_engine; #[cfg(test)] mod apctp_parse; #[cfg(test)] mod apctm_match; #[cfg(test)] mod apctd_dictionaries; ``` ## Tabtargets | Tabtarget | Colophon | Purpose | |-----------|----------|---------| | `tt/apcw-b.Build.sh` | `apcw-b` | `cargo tauri build` (release) | | `tt/apcw-r.Run.sh` | `apcw-r` | `cargo tauri dev` (local development) | | `tt/apcw-d.Deploy.sh` | `apcw-d` | Build + scp to `anns-macbook-air:/Users/Shared/apcua/` | | `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.sh` | `apcw-fl` | Run `apcal` to load fixture HTML onto clipboard | | `tt/apcw-t.Test.sh` | `apcw-t` | `cargo test` in `apcd/` |

content.text:
Yes, and it's a sharper observation than just "the spec needs updating." Let me articulate what I think the actual gap is.

The spec currently has two well-defined output contracts:

1. **Interactive triage** — findings rendered as RED/YELLOW/PASS in the frontend, toggle states, visual display
2. **Anonymization** — `apcre_anonymize` producing placeholder-substituted plain text

The batch assay is a **third output projection** of the same detection pipeline — and it's the first one that makes the engine's raw decisions *legible to a reviewer*. The interactive triage shows findings in a UI with visual affordances. The anonymization hides the PHI. The assay *exposes the classification decisions inline with the document*. That's a fundamentally different purpose: it's a diagnostic instrument, not a user-facing product.

And the moment I ran it and started reviewing the output, I was making quality judgments against criteria that don't exist in writing:

- Should "day" match as a surname? The spec says "conservative bias" but doesn't define when a dictionary collision should be a whitelist entry vs. a legitimate catch.
- Should anchored scanning bleed into timestamps? The spec says "all text until next label or line break" but the actual boundary behavior is underspecified for structured suffixes like signature timestamps.
- Should "Consulting:" be an anchor label? The spec lists specific labels but doesn't define the principle for adding new ones.
- The docket specified `‹SEVERITY:CATEGORY text›` but the spec has no concept of "assay" as an output mode.

The deeper issue is that the assay mode creates a **feedback instrument** for the detection pipeline, and without spec-level definitions for what correct output looks like, tuning is ad hoc. Each improvement becomes a judgment call in a docket rather than a measurement against a definition.

What I'd recommend adding to APCPS:

1. **Assay output mode** — format definition, purpose (diagnostic/review, not user-facing), relationship to anonymization
2. **Anchored label registry** — the principle for inclusion, not just the list (so "Consulting:" has a clear yes/no test)
3. **Anchored boundary rule** — what terminates value extraction, including the timestamp case
4. **Dictionary collision policy** — when a blacklist/whitelist collision should be resolved by whitelist addition vs. left as YELLOW

Want me to slate a pace for this spec work, or would you rather draft the definitions conversationally first?

message.content: Lets start by naming the key concepts in the prototype, at least with respect to the transform algorithm.  Not interested in output mode, and 'anchored boundary rule' seems like a step in a procedure, not a 'key concept' or entity.  thoughts?

content.text:
You're right to push back on both. Let me think about what the actual entities are.

The algorithm identifies PHI by three distinct *bases*:

1. **Structure** — the text's form matches a PHI pattern (SSN, phone, date). The text *looks like* PHI.
2. **Position** — the text follows a label that declares it PHI. The text *is situated as* PHI.
3. **Identity** — the text matches a known name or place. The text *is known as* PHI.

These are the conceptual pillars. The code calls them Regex/Anchored/Matched, but those are implementation names — the concepts are about *why* something is PHI, not *how* we found it.

Then there are entities at the intersection:

- **Collision** — a word whose identity is ambiguous (both a common word and a known name). "May" is a month and a firstname. "Wall" is a surface and a surname. The collision is a property of the word in the dictionary universe, not of any particular document.

- **Propagation** (₢A9AAN's concept, not yet built) — an entity identified by position in one location is then tracked by identity elsewhere in the same document. "Maine Medical Center" anchored at `Facility:` becomes a per-document identity. This is the bridge between position and identity — position *teaches* identity within a document.

What I notice is missing as a concept: the **scope** of positional authority. When `Electronically signed by:` claims positional authority, over *what extent of text* does that claim hold? The time-bleed bug is a scope problem — the position's claim extends past the entity it names. Scope isn't a procedure; it's an attribute of the positional claim itself.

Does this decomposition feel right, or is the grain wrong?

message.content: Its precisely the case that the terms you are using are not strong for long term specification: I'm struggling a bit to articluate why even to myself but my engineering incliations are speaking loudly.  For better or for worse, the specification needs to degenerate to concise, clear language about steps and lists of typed things to spell out how our overall algorithm works.  You did an admirable job in coding what we have, but now it is time to graduate to a more formal treatment, so that we have a solid conceptual basis for our work.  Collision and Propagation are not good for this.  On the other hand, named blacklists and named whitelists and named... what did you call them... "Anchored labels" are much better for this.  Thoughts?  Be terse for now.

content.text:
You're right. The spec should enumerate the concrete things the algorithm operates on, not name abstract patterns.

Concrete, enumerable things:
- **Label registry** — the list of labels that trigger positional extraction (Patient:, Attending:, Facility:, ...)
- **Blacklist dictionaries** — surnames, firstnames, cities (each named, each with source and size)
- **Whitelist dictionaries** — english, medical (each named)
- **Regex patterns** — the table of structural patterns (SSN, phone, date, ...)
- **Category set** — the PHI types (NAME, PROVIDER, FACILITY, ...)
- **Severity levels** — RED, YELLOW, PASS (with defined meaning)

What I was calling "collision" is just the lookup result when a word appears in both a blacklist and a whitelist. That's a row in a truth table, not a concept. What I was calling "propagation" is a proposed step in the algorithm that doesn't exist yet — premature to name.

The spec should read like: "Here are the dictionaries. Here are the labels. Here are the patterns. Here is the order of operations. Here is the merge rule." Each thing named, typed, and enumerable.

message.content: Better, but far from ideal: 'category set' makes my scalp itch for its vagueness.  I'm now thinking we need to go ahead and create an asciidoc core document like RBS0 for this with MCM and AXLA terms, though it will be much smaller: we'll need to come up with quoins for each of the tables and the procedures we use for scanning the document and then processing each of the sections to emit the final result.

content.text:
Agreed. A concept model gives each term an anchor — no more "category set" handwaving.

Before I build, the mint question: what prefix and document name? Options:

1. `APCPD` — APCK Pipeline Detection (new doc in `apck/` or `apck/vov_veiled/`)
2. Fold into APCPS as a concept model section (but APCPS is markdown, not adoc)
3. New `apcp_` category for pipeline quoins inside a new adoc

And the quoin categories I'd expect to mint:

- `apcp_` — pipeline entities (the dictionaries, the label registry, the regex table, the severity levels, the finding)
- Or split: `apcd_` for detection resources, `apcp_` for pipeline steps — but `apcd` is already taken (Rust source directory)

The quoins themselves would name things like: "the surname dictionary", "the label registry", "a finding", "RED severity", each PHI category — not "collision" or "propagation."

What's your instinct on the prefix and document placement?

So:

toolUseResult.originalFile:
## Ann's PHI Clipbuddy Kit (APCK)

APCK is a Tauri desktop app that intercepts clipboard content from Epic EHR, detects PHI via a three-tier engine (regex, label-anchored, dictionary), presents a red/yellow/grey triage view, and writes anonymized plain text to clipboard for pasting into Open Evidence.

### File Acronym Mappings

| Acronym | File | Description |
|---------|------|-------------|
| **APCS** | `apck/APCS-Specification.md` | Product spec — UX, workflow, deployment |
| **APCPS** | `apck/APCPS-PrototypeSpecification.md` | Prototype spec — detection pipeline, tech stack |
| **APCW** | `apck/apcw_workbench.sh` | Workbench |
| **APCZ** | `apck/apcz_zipper.sh` | Zipper enrollment |
| **APCC** | `apck/apcc_cli.sh` | CLI command implementations |
| **APCAP** | `apck/apcd/src/apcap_main.rs` | Tauri app entry point |
| **APCAL** | `apck/apcd/src/apcal_main.rs` | Fixture loader (clipboard writer) |
| **APCAD** | `apck/apcd/src/apcad_main.rs` | Dictionary refresh (downloads public sources, regenerates dictionaries) |
| **APCAB** | `apck/apcd/src/apcab_main.rs` | Batch assay (run detection pipeline on HTML directory, write assay output) |

**Source modules:**

| Source | Prefix | Test | Prefix | Purpose |
|--------|--------|------|--------|---------|
| `apcrl_log.rs` | `apcrl` | — | — | Logging macros (`apcrl_info!`, `apcrl_error!`, `apcrl_fatal!`) |
| `apcre_engine.rs` | `apcre` | `apcte_engine.rs` | `apcte` | PHI detection orchestrator |
| `apcrp_parse.rs` | `apcrp` | `apctp_parse.rs` | `apctp` | HTML clipboard parsing |
| `apcrm_match.rs` | `apcrm` | `apctm_match.rs` | `apctm` | Dictionary/regex matching |
| `apcrd_dictionaries.rs` | `apcrd` | `apctd_dictionaries.rs` | `apctd` | Dictionary loading |
| `apcru_update.rs` | `apcru` | — | — | Self-update watcher (no unit tests — I/O + process) |

**Other key paths:**
- `Tools/apck/apcd/ui/` — Frontend (HTML/CSS only — no JavaScript)
- `Tools/apck/apcd/dictionaries/` — Blacklist/whitelist data files
- `Tools/apck/test_fixtures/` — Synthetic Epic clipboard data

**No JavaScript rule:** All rendering logic lives in Rust. The Tauri webview is a passive display surface — Rust pushes complete HTML on every state change via `eval()`. Inline `onclick` attributes use the Tauri bridge primitive (`window.__TAURI__.core.invoke`) for command dispatch. No `.js` files, no JS application logic. This eliminates the Rust-JS language boundary as a bug class.

**Rust conventions:** Follow RCG (`Tools/vok/vov_veiled/RCG-RustCodingGuide.md`). Source prefix: `apcr{classifier}_{name}.rs`. Test prefix: `apct{classifier}_{name}.rs`. Classifier matches between source and test.

### Tabtargets

| Tabtarget | Colophon | Purpose |
|-----------|----------|---------|
| `tt/apcw-b.Build.sh` | `apcw-b` | `cargo tauri build` (release) |
| `tt/apcw-r.Run.sh` | `apcw-r` | `cargo run --bin apcap` (local development) |
| `tt/apcw-d.Deploy.sh` | `apcw-d` | Build + scp to `anns-macbook-air:/Users/Shared/apcua/` |
| `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.sh` | `apcw-fl` | Run `apcal` to load fixture HTML onto clipboard |
| `tt/apcw-t.Test.sh` | `apcw-t` | `cargo test` in `apcd/` |
| `tt/apcw-dr.DictionaryRefresh.sh` | `apcw-dr` | `cargo run --bin apcad` (refresh dictionaries from public sources) |
| `tt/apcw-ba.BatchAssay.sh` | `apcw-ba` | `cargo run --bin apcab` (batch assay on HTML directory) |

### Prefix Tree

```
apc  (non-terminal)
├── apca   (non-terminal)
│   ├── apcab  — App Batch binary (assay — detection pipeline on HTML files)
│   ├── apcad  — App Dictionary binary (refresh from public sources)
│   ├── apcal  — App Loader binary (fixture clipboard tool)
│   └── apcap  — App Prototype binary (Tauri main)
├── apcc   — CLI command implementations
├── apcd   — Rust/Tauri source directory
│   └── apcrl  — Logging macros (info, error, fatal with file/line)
├── apck   — kit directory
├── apcps  — prototype specification document
├── apcs   — product specification document
├── apcu   (non-terminal)
│   └── apcua — update staging directory (/Users/Shared/apcua/)
├── apcw   — workbench
└── apcz   — zipper
```

### Detection Architecture

Three tiers, merged by highest severity:
- **Tier 1 — Regex (RED):** SSN, phone, email, dates, addresses, zip, labeled identifiers
- **Tier 2 — Label-anchored (RED):** Words following Epic labels (Patient:, Attending:, Facility:, etc.)
- **Tier 3 — Dictionary (YELLOW/PASS):** aho-corasick blacklist scan, whitelist filter, collision → YELLOW

### Deploy Workflow

1. `tt/apcw-b.Build.sh` — produces `.app` bundle
2. `tt/apcw-d.Deploy.sh` — scp to Ann's machine at `/Users/Shared/apcua/`
3. App self-updates: watches staging directory, copies new bundle over self, relaunches

content.content:
1	## Character
2	
3	Product design into prototype implementation. Starts with synthesis (specs capturing a rich design conversation), transitions to exploratory engineering (Tauri + Rust + cross-platform clipboard), then steady implementation of a three-tier PHI detection engine. Requires careful attention to minting discipline — this kit lives alongside RBK/JJK/BUK and must respect the prefix universe.
4	
5	## Problem
6	
7	Clinicians using Epic EHR need to consult Open Evidence (an AI diagnostic tool) but must not paste Protected Health Information (PHI) into it. HIPAA Safe Harbor defines 18 PHI categories that must be removed. Today, clinicians either skip the consultation or manually scrub text — both are unacceptable. No clipboard-level tool exists to automate the triage.
8	
9	## Solution
10	
11	Ann's PHI Clipbuddy (`apcap`) — a Tauri desktop app (macOS, later Windows) that:
12	1. Validates clipboard content as clinical via heuristic, clears system clipboard after consumption
13	2. Parses Epic "Copy All" HTML for structural context (labels, sections)
14	3. Runs a three-tier PHI detection engine (regex → label-anchored → dictionary)
15	4. Displays a triage view: RED (definite PHI), YELLOW (questionable), GREY (pass)
16	5. Lets clinician toggle findings — YELLOW easily, RED with deliberate friction (separate panel section)
17	6. Writes anonymized plain text to clipboard with category-specific placeholders (DOB→age, >89→"90+")
18	7. Self-updates from `/Users/Shared/apcua/` without confirmation
19	
20	## Key Design Decisions
21	
22	- **No JavaScript**: All rendering logic is Rust. The webview is a passive display surface. Decided during ₢A9AAP after the byte-vs-char offset mismatch bug (₢A9AAK) demonstrated the cost of a two-language boundary in a HIPAA-adjacent tool. Policy documented in APCPS scope boundaries, APCS product vision, and apck-claude-context.md.
23	- **Anchored vs Matched**: Tier 2 (label position) and Tier 3 (dictionary identity) are independent mechanisms with distinct vocabulary — see APCPS
24	- **Case-insensitive**: All dictionary lookups normalize to lowercase; original case preserved in display
25	- **Per-word anchoring**: No multi-word merge — each word individually classified
26	- **Conservative bias**: False positive preferred over false negative
27	- **Provider/facility names**: Treated as PHI (re-identifying in combination with clinical details)
28	- **Clipboard always cleared** after consumption
29	- **Clinical content heuristic** gates processing; non-clinical clipboard shows diagnostic and clears triage
30	
31	## Authoritative Documents
32	
33	| Document | Owns | Path |
34	|----------|------|------|
35	| Product Spec (APCS) | UX plan, workflow, deployment, HIPAA context | `Tools/apck/APCS-Specification.md` |
36	| Prototype Spec (APCPS) | Detection pipeline, tech stack, dictionaries, project structure, scope boundaries | `Tools/apck/APCPS-PrototypeSpecification.md` |
37	| Claude Context | Build/run/deploy instructions, file mappings, prefix tree, tabtargets | `Tools/apck/apck-claude-context.md` |
38	| Rust Coding Guide (RCG) | Naming conventions, test patterns, disciplines | `Tools/vok/vov_veiled/RCG-RustCodingGuide.md` |

file.content:
## Character

Product design into prototype implementation. Starts with synthesis (specs capturing a rich design conversation), transitions to exploratory engineering (Tauri + Rust + cross-platform clipboard), then steady implementation of a three-tier PHI detection engine. Requires careful attention to minting discipline — this kit lives alongside RBK/JJK/BUK and must respect the prefix universe.

## Problem

Clinicians using Epic EHR need to consult Open Evidence (an AI diagnostic tool) but must not paste Protected Health Information (PHI) into it. HIPAA Safe Harbor defines 18 PHI categories that must be removed. Today, clinicians either skip the consultation or manually scrub text — both are unacceptable. No clipboard-level tool exists to automate the triage.

## Solution

Ann's PHI Clipbuddy (`apcap`) — a Tauri desktop app (macOS, later Windows) that:
1. Validates clipboard content as clinical via heuristic, clears system clipboard after consumption
2. Parses Epic "Copy All" HTML for structural context (labels, sections)
3. Runs a three-tier PHI detection engine (regex → label-anchored → dictionary)
4. Displays a triage view: RED (definite PHI), YELLOW (questionable), GREY (pass)
5. Lets clinician toggle findings — YELLOW easily, RED with deliberate friction (separate panel section)
6. Writes anonymized plain text to clipboard with category-specific placeholders (DOB→age, >89→"90+")
7. Self-updates from `/Users/Shared/apcua/` without confirmation

## Key Design Decisions

- **No JavaScript**: All rendering logic is Rust. The webview is a passive display surface. Decided during ₢A9AAP after the byte-vs-char offset mismatch bug (₢A9AAK) demonstrated the cost of a two-language boundary in a HIPAA-adjacent tool. Policy documented in APCPS scope boundaries, APCS product vision, and apck-claude-context.md.
- **Anchored vs Matched**: Tier 2 (label position) and Tier 3 (dictionary identity) are independent mechanisms with distinct vocabulary — see APCPS
- **Case-insensitive**: All dictionary lookups normalize to lowercase; original case preserved in display
- **Per-word anchoring**: No multi-word merge — each word individually classified
- **Conservative bias**: False positive preferred over false negative
- **Provider/facility names**: Treated as PHI (re-identifying in combination with clinical details)
- **Clipboard always cleared** after consumption
- **Clinical content heuristic** gates processing; non-clinical clipboard shows diagnostic and clears triage

## Authoritative Documents

| Document | Owns | Path |
|----------|------|------|
| Product Spec (APCS) | UX plan, workflow, deployment, HIPAA context | `Tools/apck/APCS-Specification.md` |
| Prototype Spec (APCPS) | Detection pipeline, tech stack, dictionaries, project structure, scope boundaries | `Tools/apck/APCPS-PrototypeSpecification.md` |
| Claude Context | Build/run/deploy instructions, file mappings, prefix tree, tabtargets | `Tools/apck/apck-claude-context.md` |
| Rust Coding Guide (RCG) | Naming conventions, test patterns, disciplines | `Tools/vok/vov_veiled/RCG-RustCodingGuide.md` |

14	// axi_: Axial Interface (interface patterns) → mcm_form_deflist

= Ann's PHI Clipbuddy — Detection Pipeline Specification
:toc:
:toc-title: Table of Contents
:sectnums:

// tag::mapping-section[]
//
// Category declarations:
// apcs_: APCK Specification (detection pipeline concepts)
//
// Variant patterns:
//   _s: plural form
//   _p: possessive form
//   _ed: past tense (verbs)
//   _ing: progressive form (verbs)

// Input
:apcs_clipboard_content:           <<apcs_clipboard_content,Clipboard Content>>
:apcs_epic_html:                   <<apcs_epic_html,Epic HTML>>
:apcs_clinical_gate:               <<apcs_clinical_gate,Clinical Gate>>

// Parsed structure
:apcs_document:                    <<apcs_document,Parsed Document>>
:apcs_plain_text:                  <<apcs_plain_text,Plain Text>>
:apcs_span:                        <<apcs_span,Span>>
:apcs_span_s:                      <<apcs_span,Spans>>
:apcs_labeled_field:               <<apcs_labeled_field,Labeled Field>>
:apcs_labeled_field_s:             <<apcs_labeled_field,Labeled Fields>>
:apcs_section_header:              <<apcs_section_header,Section Header>>
:apcs_section_header_s:            <<apcs_section_header,Section Headers>>
:apcs_narrative:                   <<apcs_narrative,Narrative>>

// Detection resources — registries and dictionaries
:apcs_label_registry:              <<apcs_label_registry,Label Registry>>
:apcs_regex_table:                 <<apcs_regex_table,Regex Table>>
:apcs_surname_dictionary:          <<apcs_surname_dictionary,Surname Dictionary>>
:apcs_firstname_dictionary:        <<apcs_firstname_dictionary,Firstname Dictionary>>
:apcs_city_dictionary:             <<apcs_city_dictionary,City Dictionary>>
:apcs_blacklist:                   <<apcs_blacklist,Blacklist>>
:apcs_blacklist_s:                 <<apcs_blacklist,Blacklists>>
:apcs_english_whitelist:           <<apcs_english_whitelist,English Whitelist>>
:apcs_medical_whitelist:           <<apcs_medical_whitelist,Medical Whitelist>>
:apcs_whitelist:                   <<apcs_whitelist,Whitelist>>
:apcs_whitelist_s:                 <<apcs_whitelist,Whitelists>>

// Finding anatomy
:apcs_finding:                     <<apcs_finding,Finding>>
:apcs_finding_s:                   <<apcs_finding,Findings>>
:apcs_severity:                    <<apcs_severity,Severity>>
:apcs_red:                         <<apcs_red,RED>>
:apcs_yellow:                      <<apcs_yellow,YELLOW>>
:apcs_pass:                        <<apcs_pass,PASS>>
:apcs_phi_category:                <<apcs_phi_category,PHI Category>>
:apcs_phi_category_ies:            <<apcs_phi_category,PHI Categories>>

// Pipeline steps
:apcs_parse:                       <<apcs_parse,Parse>>
:apcs_regex_scan:                  <<apcs_regex_scan,Regex Scan>>
:apcs_label_scan:                  <<apcs_label_scan,Label Scan>>
:apcs_dictionary_scan:             <<apcs_dictionary_scan,Dictionary Scan>>
:apcs_merge:                       <<apcs_merge,Merge>>

// Output projections
:apcs_anonymized_text:             <<apcs_anonymized_text,Anonymized Text>>
:apcs_assay:                       <<apcs_assay,Assay>>
:apcs_elision_marker:              <<apcs_elision_marker,Elision Marker>>
:apcs_elision_marker_s:            <<apcs_elision_marker,Elision Markers>>
:apcs_placeholder:                 <<apcs_placeholder,Placeholder>>
:apcs_placeholder_s:               <<apcs_placeholder,Placeholders>>

// end::mapping-section[]


== Overview

This document defines the formal vocabulary for the detection pipeline in Ann's PHI Clipbuddy.
It specifies the concrete entities the algorithm operates on, the resources it consults, and the steps it performs.

The application specification (APCAS) owns the user experience.
The prototype specification (APCPS) owns implementation details, tech stack, and data sources.
This document owns the detection algorithm's conceptual structure — the terms that must be precise for the pipeline to be reasoned about correctly.


== Input

[[apcs_clipboard_content]]
{apcs_clipboard_content}::
Raw content read from the system clipboard on window focus.
May be HTML or plain text.
Consumed once, then the system clipboard is cleared.

[[apcs_epic_html]]
{apcs_epic_html}::
HTML produced by Epic's "Copy All" function.
Semi-structured: bold labels for demographics, section headers for clinical categories, narrative freetext for clinical prose, signature blocks for provider attribution.
The structural formatting is the primary advantage over plain text — labels anchor PHI detection with high precision.

[[apcs_clinical_gate]]
{apcs_clinical_gate}::
A heuristic that decides whether
{apcs_clipboard_content}
is clinical.
Scans for known label patterns (Patient:, DOB:, MRN:, etc.).
Acceptance threshold: two or more recognized labels present.
Content that fails the
{apcs_clinical_gate}
is not processed; the triage view clears.


== Parsed Structure

[[apcs_document]]
{apcs_document}::
The result of parsing
{apcs_epic_html}.
Contains a sequence of typed
{apcs_span_s}
and the derived
{apcs_plain_text}.

[[apcs_plain_text]]
{apcs_plain_text}::
A single string derived from the
{apcs_document}
by stripping HTML markup, normalizing whitespace, and concatenating span contributions with structural line breaks.
All detection scans operate against
{apcs_plain_text}.
Offsets in
{apcs_finding_s}
are byte positions within this string.

[[apcs_span]]
{apcs_span}::
A typed region within the
{apcs_document}.
Each
{apcs_span}
has a kind, an optional label, text content, and a byte offset into
{apcs_plain_text}.
Three kinds:
{apcs_labeled_field},
{apcs_section_header},
{apcs_narrative}.

[[apcs_labeled_field]]
{apcs_labeled_field}::
A
{apcs_span}
produced by a bold label followed by a value (e.g., `Patient: Margaret J. Thornton`).
The label is the bold text before the colon.
The value is the text after the colon, extending to the next structural boundary (another label, line break, or HTML element boundary).

[[apcs_section_header]]
{apcs_section_header}::
A
{apcs_span}
produced by a bold label matching a known clinical section name (Chief Complaint, History of Present Illness, Assessment/Plan, etc.).
Contributes the header text to
{apcs_plain_text}
but carries no value — the section body follows as subsequent
{apcs_span_s}.

[[apcs_narrative]]
{apcs_narrative}::
A
{apcs_span}
containing freetext not attached to a label.
Clinical prose, family history details, plan items.
PHI in
{apcs_narrative}
{apcs_span_s}
lacks positional anchoring and must be detected by the
{apcs_regex_scan}
or
{apcs_dictionary_scan}.


== Detection Resources

=== Registries

[[apcs_label_registry]]
{apcs_label_registry}::
The enumerated set of
{apcs_labeled_field}
labels that trigger per-word PHI extraction in the
{apcs_label_scan}.
Each entry specifies the label text (case-insensitive match) and the
{apcs_phi_category}
assigned to extracted words.
+
[cols="2,1"]
|===
| Label | Category

| Patient:           | NAME
| Attending:         | PROVIDER
| Provider:          | PROVIDER
| Referring:         | PROVIDER
| Facility:          | FACILITY
| Electronically signed by: | PROVIDER
| Emergency Contact  | NAME
| Primary Care Physician | PROVIDER
|===

[[apcs_regex_table]]
{apcs_regex_table}::
The enumerated set of regular expression patterns that detect structurally identifiable PHI in the
{apcs_regex_scan}.
Each entry specifies a pattern, a
{apcs_phi_category},
and a capture group index.
All matches produce
{apcs_red}
{apcs_finding_s}.
See APCPS for the full pattern table.

=== Dictionaries

[[apcs_blacklist]]
{apcs_blacklist}::
A word list whose members are flagged as potential PHI during the
{apcs_dictionary_scan}.
Three named
{apcs_blacklist_s}
are combined into a single lookup automaton:

[[apcs_surname_dictionary]]
{apcs_surname_dictionary}::
US Census 2010 surnames, top 1,000 by frequency.
Source: public domain.

[[apcs_firstname_dictionary]]
{apcs_firstname_dictionary}::
SSA baby names, top 1,000 by aggregate frequency.
Source: public domain.

[[apcs_city_dictionary]]
{apcs_city_dictionary}::
US state capitals plus top 100 cities by population.
Source: public domain.

[[apcs_whitelist]]
{apcs_whitelist}::
A word list whose members are common English or medical terms.
Membership in a
{apcs_whitelist}
does not prevent a
{apcs_finding} —
a word in both a
{apcs_blacklist}
and a
{apcs_whitelist}
is classified
{apcs_yellow}.
Two named
{apcs_whitelist_s}:

[[apcs_english_whitelist]]
{apcs_english_whitelist}::
Top 5,000 common English words (Norvig/Google n-grams).
Source: public domain.

[[apcs_medical_whitelist]]
{apcs_medical_whitelist}::
Hand-curated medications, lab tests, diagnoses, anatomy, abbreviations.
Source: original curation.


== Finding Anatomy

[[apcs_finding]]
{apcs_finding}::
A classified region of
{apcs_plain_text}.
Produced by one of the three detection scans.
Each
{apcs_finding}
carries: the matched text, a byte offset and length in
{apcs_plain_text},
a
{apcs_severity},
a
{apcs_phi_category},
and a replacement string for anonymization.

[[apcs_severity]]
{apcs_severity}::
The confidence level of a
{apcs_finding}.
Determines default elision behavior and visual presentation.
Three levels:

[[apcs_red]]
{apcs_red}::
Definite PHI.
Produced by the
{apcs_regex_scan}
(structural match) or the
{apcs_label_scan}
(positional authority).
Default: elide.

[[apcs_yellow]]
{apcs_yellow}::
Possible PHI.
Produced by the
{apcs_dictionary_scan}
when a word appears in a
{apcs_blacklist}.
Default: elide.

[[apcs_pass]]
{apcs_pass}::
Not PHI.
A word appearing only in a
{apcs_whitelist}
and not in any
{apcs_blacklist}.
No
{apcs_finding}
is emitted for
{apcs_pass}
words.

[[apcs_phi_category]]
{apcs_phi_category}::
The HIPAA Safe Harbor category of a
{apcs_finding}.
Determines the
{apcs_placeholder}
text used in
{apcs_anonymized_text}.
Enumerated: NAME, PROVIDER, FACILITY, DATE, DOB, PHONE, EMAIL, SSN, MRN, ACCOUNT, ENCOUNTER_ID, ADDRESS, HEALTH_PLAN_ID, DEVICE_ID, NPI, DEA, URL, IP_ADDRESS.


== Pipeline Steps

The detection pipeline executes five steps in fixed order.
Each step either transforms input or produces
{apcs_finding_s}.

[[apcs_parse]]
{apcs_parse}::
Transform
{apcs_epic_html}
into a
{apcs_document}:
a sequence of typed
{apcs_span_s}
and derived
{apcs_plain_text}.
HTML structure is consumed; output is position-mapped text.

[[apcs_regex_scan]]
{apcs_regex_scan}::
Scan
{apcs_plain_text}
against every pattern in the
{apcs_regex_table}.
Each match emits a
{apcs_red}
{apcs_finding}
with the
{apcs_phi_category}
specified by the pattern.

[[apcs_label_scan]]
{apcs_label_scan}::
For each
{apcs_labeled_field}
whose label appears in the
{apcs_label_registry}:
extract the value text, segment into words, and emit one
{apcs_red}
{apcs_finding}
per word with the
{apcs_phi_category}
specified by the registry entry.

[[apcs_dictionary_scan]]
{apcs_dictionary_scan}::
Scan
{apcs_plain_text}
against the combined
{apcs_blacklist}
automaton.
For each hit, check
{apcs_whitelist}
membership.
Emit a
{apcs_yellow}
{apcs_finding}
if the word is in any
{apcs_blacklist}
(regardless of
{apcs_whitelist}
membership).
Words in a
{apcs_whitelist}
only receive no
{apcs_finding}.

[[apcs_merge]]
{apcs_merge}::
Combine all
{apcs_finding_s}
from the three scans.
When multiple
{apcs_finding_s}
occupy the same byte range, retain only the one with highest
{apcs_severity}
({apcs_red} > {apcs_yellow} > {apcs_pass}).


== Output Projections

[[apcs_anonymized_text]]
{apcs_anonymized_text}::
{apcs_plain_text}
with elided
{apcs_finding_s}
replaced by their
{apcs_placeholder_s}.
Findings toggled to
{apcs_pass}
by the clinician pass through with original text.
The output format for pasting into Open Evidence.

[[apcs_placeholder]]
{apcs_placeholder}::
A category-specific substitution string used in
{apcs_anonymized_text}.
Format: `[CATEGORY]` (e.g., `[NAME]`, `[PHONE]`, `[ADDRESS]`).
Exception: DOB emits `Age: NN` (computed from date of birth and current date; ages over 89 emit `Age: 90+` per HIPAA Safe Harbor).

[[apcs_assay]]
{apcs_assay}::
{apcs_plain_text}
with
{apcs_finding_s}
annotated inline using
{apcs_elision_marker_s}.
A diagnostic projection — shows the detection decisions within the document for review.
Not user-facing; used for pipeline quality assessment.

[[apcs_elision_marker]]
{apcs_elision_marker}::
The annotation unit in an
{apcs_assay}.
Format: `‹SEVERITY:CATEGORY matched text›` (guillemet-delimited).
Shows the
{apcs_severity},
{apcs_phi_category},
and original text of each
{apcs_finding}
inline with surrounding
{apcs_plain_text}.
